slides.heading: Humans already figured this out — centuries ago 🎨
slides.body:
Before we talk about software, let's talk about something you already know.

A sculptor carving a stone figure doesn't do it in one go. After each important stage, they step back, look at it, take a photograph, sometimes make a plaster cast.

A writer doesn't write a novel in one draft. They save copies at the end of every chapter. Every major edit.

These aren't versions of the file. They're **snapshots of progress**.

And here's the key insight: they always keep the last snapshot safe before making the next big change.
bullets: 📸 A sculptor's photo = a moment frozen in time
📝 A writer's saved draft = a point they can return to
🎨 A designer's version 3 = a checkpoint
💡 Code can work exactly the same way

components.hint: Tap each creative field — see how they already handle versions

items.label: Painters
items.detail: Photographers take a photo of the canvas after each major stage. They can always show you what it looked like at step 3 — even if it looks totally different now.

items.label: Authors
items.detail: Writers save a new copy of their manuscript at the end of each draft. 'novel-draft-3.docx' is a checkpoint they can return to if they write themselves into a corner.

items.label: Architects
items.detail: Architects keep every version of a blueprint. Client wanted a wall moved? They don't scrap the old blueprint — they keep it and create a new version.

items.label: Game players
items.detail: You know save points in games? That's literally the same idea as a commit. The game saves your exact state — health, items, position — so you can return to it.

slides.heading: Idea: What if you could take a photo of your code? 📸
slides.body:
Imagine if every time your code was in a good state, you could press a button.

That button *freezes* the exact state of every file. Labels it with a message — "working todo app with checkboxes" — and stores it safely forever.

From that moment on, you can always go back to that exact state. Even if you accidentally delete everything. Even after a hundred more changes.

That frozen moment? That's what version control calls a **commit**.

Let's watch Kavya use this idea — click through the journey below.
bullets: 📸 Snapshot = commit (a frozen moment of your code)
💬 Every snapshot has a label explaining what it captured
🔒 Once taken, a snapshot can never be lost
↩️ You can return to any snapshot, any time

components.hint: Click through — watch Kavya use snapshots correctly this time

steps.result: 📸 Kavya takes a snapshot! Message: 'Working todo app — checkboxes working'
She edits freely — the previous snapshot is safe, no matter what she does now
It's broken. But she's not panicking — she has a snapshot from before.

steps.action: She goes back to the snapshot
steps.result: ✅ ONE command — her working todo app is back. Exactly as it was. Dark mode experiment gone.

slides.heading: This solves Problem #1! ✅
slides.body:
Remember Kavya's first disaster?

She added dark mode. Everything broke. The working version was gone because she saved over it.

**With snapshots:**
Before starting dark mode, Kavya takes a snapshot — "todo app working perfectly."

She tries dark mode. It breaks. No problem.
She goes back to the snapshot. The working version is right there.

She didn't have to remember what the code looked like. She didn't have to undo 40 times. One command, and she's back.

See the difference below — same situation, two outcomes.
bullets: Before: "Save" overwrites. No going back.
After: "Commit" takes a snapshot. Always can go back.
🎯 The snapshot is permanent — even deleting the file won't lose it
→ Try the interactive comparison below!

left.title: Without snapshots
revealContent: Ctrl+Z goes too far — loses working code too
😭 2 hours of work gone, plus the original

right.title: With snapshots
revealContent: She types one command to go back
✅ Zero work lost. The experiment didn't cost her anything.

slides.heading: But snapshots are useless without good labels 🏷️
slides.body:
Here's where Kavya's second problem has its answer.

Remember her folder of doom? Files named like:
- todo-copy-backup-v2-REAL.js
- todo-WORKING-VERSION-final.js

Those are snapshots. But terrible ones — because the names tell you nothing useful.

With version control, every snapshot gets a *real label*: a message you write that explains exactly what changed.

Tap each example below to see the difference between a bad label and a good one.
bullets: ❌ Bad label: 'final-v2-copy-USE_THIS'
❌ Bad label: 'changes'
✅ Good label: 'Fix checkbox disappearing on mobile'
✅ Good label: 'Add dark mode toggle — light mode still default'

components.hint: Tap each label — see why good labels change everything

items.label: todo-backup-WORKING-v2-copy
items.detail: You have no idea what's in this. When was it? What features does it have? What was the last thing changed? Completely useless as a label.

items.label: changes
items.detail: What changes?! This could be literally anything. You'd have to open the file and compare it manually to figure out what 'changes' means.

items.label: Fix: checkboxes disappearing on mobile screens
items.detail: Perfect. Anyone reading this immediately knows: this snapshot fixed a specific bug. If that bug appears again, this is the snapshot to look at.

items.label: Add: search bar for filtering tasks
items.detail: Clear. Descriptive. You know exactly what was added and where. Finding this snapshot in a year? Easy.

slides.heading: Snapshots + Labels = a timeline of your project 🗓️
slides.body:
When you have many snapshots, each with a clear label, something magical happens.

You have a *complete history* of your project.

Every decision. Every change. Every feature added. Every bug fixed.

You can scroll through that history like reading a diary. You can jump to any moment instantly. You can see *exactly* what was different between any two moments.

This solves Problem #2 completely. No more folder of doom.

Click through Kavya's timeline below and see how clean it looks.
bullets: 📅 Most recent snapshot at the top
📜 Scroll back through the entire life of a project
🔍 Click any snapshot to see exactly what changed
🧭 Find the moment a bug appeared in seconds

components.hint: This is what Kavya's project history looks like with good labels — click any snapshot

commits.detail: The very first snapshot. Everything from this moment is saved forever.

commits.message: Add: checkbox to mark tasks done
commits.detail: Second snapshot. Clear label — you know exactly what this adds.
Third snapshot. A bug fix. If this bug ever comes back, this is the snapshot to look at.

commits.message: Add: delete button for each task
commits.detail: Most recent snapshot. HEAD points here — this is where you are now.

components.tip: No more folder of doom. One project. A clean labelled history.

slides.heading: Last problem: what about working with others? 🤝
slides.body:
Kavya and Rohan still have a problem. They both have their own snapshots — but on different computers.

Here's the solution: a **shared storage place online**.

Imagine a shared Google Drive, but specifically for code snapshots.

Kavya pushes her snapshots there. Rohan pushes his snapshots there. When they fetch each other's changes, version control figures out how to combine them.

If they changed different parts, it merges automatically. If they changed the same part, it flags it and asks them to decide.

That shared place is called a **remote**. GitHub is the most popular one.
bullets: ☁️ Remote = a shared central copy online
📤 push = send your snapshots to the shared place
📥 pull = get others' snapshots from the shared place
🔀 merge = combine different people's work intelligently

components.hint: Tap to explore the solution for Problem #3 — working with others

items.label: A shared place online (Remote)
items.detail: Imagine a shared Google Drive — but just for code snapshots. Kavya and Rohan both connect to the same place. Their changes are stored separately until deliberately merged.

items.label: Push — send your snapshots
items.detail: When Kavya's search feature is ready, she pushes her snapshots to the shared place. Rohan can see exactly what she changed and when.

items.label: Pull — get others' snapshots
items.detail: When Rohan pushes his filter feature, Kavya pulls it. Git combines their changes — automatically, if they edited different parts. If they edited the same line, it asks them to decide.

items.label: Merge — combine intelligently
items.detail: Git doesn't just overwrite. It looks at what each person changed and merges them together. Kavya's search + Rohan's filters = both in the project, safely combined.
